lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: But who did bid thee join with us?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: He needs not our mistrust, since he delivers

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Our offices and what we have to do

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: The west yet glimmers with some streaks of day:

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Now spurs the lated traveller apace

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: To gain the timely inn; and near approaches

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Give us a light there, ho!

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: That are within the note of expectation

lines.speaker: THIRD MURDERER
lines.text: Almost a mile: but he does usually,

lines.speaker: THIRD MURDERER
lines.text: So all men do, from hence to the palace gate

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Let it come down. They set upon Banquo.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: O, treachery! Fly, good Fleance, fly, fly, fly!

lines.speaker: THIRD MURDERER
lines.text: Who did strike out the light?

lines.speaker: THIRD MURDERER
lines.text: There’s but one down; the son is fled.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Well, let’s away, and say how much is done.

stage_directions.text: Enter Banquo, and Fleance with a torch.

glosses.definition: a late traveler caught by nightfall

glosses.phrase: Fly, good Fleance, fly, fly, fly!
glosses.definition: Banquo's dying command — and the prophecy's preservation; Fleance's escape means the Stuart line will eventually reach the throne
the more important part of our task — killing Fleance — is lost

annotations.title: Fleance escapes
annotations.body:
The murder of Banquo is staged; the escape of Fleance is offstage but louder. 'Fly, good Fleance, fly, fly, fly!' — the dying father's command preserves the prophecy. The third murderer (whose identity is never explained — some editors suspect Macbeth himself, disguised) lets the boy go by accident.

Fleance's flight is structurally the whole point. If Macbeth had succeeded, the Stuart line would have been aborted. James I's claim to the throne ran through Banquo (via the historical Fleance) to Robert II and the Stuart kings. The audience at the 1606 performance knew that the escaping boy was, in political time, their own king's ancestor. The tragedy of Macbeth is also the legitimation narrative of the reigning monarch.

The scene is short — 22 lines — but its consequence dominates the rest of the play and much of British political history.
sources: Henry N. Paul, *The Royal Play of Macbeth* (1950)

options: He preserves the Banquo-Stuart line — James I claimed descent from Banquo; the prophecy is still in force

trials.prompt: A third murderer appears unexpectedly. Editorial speculation sometimes identifies him as:
options: Macbeth himself, disguised — a persistent interpretive theory
